commonAnnotations.summary: {{SVC}} consumer backlog grew to 800k messages (SLO 50k) in {{NS}}
commonAnnotations.description: app_consumer_lag_k for {{WORKLOAD}} (the notif-queue Redis list) climbed from a steady ~8k to 800k messages, ~16x over the 50k SLO. Producers are enqueuing far faster than the 3 consumer replicas can drain: the per-replica consume rate is pinned at its single-threaded ceiling (~1.6k msgs/s each, ~4.9k/s total) while the inbound enqueue rate is ~14k msgs/s after an upstream producer flood. The backlog and end-to-end notification delivery latency keep growing. Consumer pods are Ready with 0 restarts, CPU/memory nominal, nodes Ready, and the deployment revision is unchanged — this is throughput under-provisioning, not a crash, OOM, bad deploy, or broker outage.
commonAnnotations.error: ConsumerLag: notifications backlog grew to 800k msgs

adversarial_signals: End-to-end notification delivery latency p99 is badly elevated (~42s vs 1.8s baseline), which could look like a slow downstream dependency_failure on the notif-queue/Redis broker — but Redis itself responds in <2ms and the latency is purely queue-wait time, not slow processing.

Consumer CPU is at the top of its band (~0.46 / 0.5 core limit, ~92% with ~6% throttle), tempting a resource_exhaustion / 'add more CPU' conclusion — but each replica is already draining at its max single-threaded rate; the bottleneck is replica count (parallelism), not CPU headroom, and raising the CPU limit would not add consumer parallelism.

The notif-queue Redis pod shows a Normal 'Started' event ~9m before the incident, which could look like a Redis restart/crash that dropped consumer connections and stalled draining — but consumer connections are continuous, LLEN history shows no gap, and the backlog growth begins at the producer flood, not the restart.

{{SVC}} request_rate (producer enqueue) is high (~14k msgs/s), which can read as an organic-traffic saturation that will self-resolve — but it persists above consumer capacity, so the lag grows monotonically and will not drain without more consumers.

pods.name: {{POD}}
pods.note: Ready, 0 restarts, single-threaded consumer (CONSUMER_CONCURRENCY=1) draining at its max rate — the failure is throughput under-provisioning, not a crash or OOM

events.message: app_consumer_lag_k for {{WORKLOAD}} reached 800 (800k msgs) against a 50k SLO; enqueue ~14k/s vs total consume ~4.9k/s — backlog growing ~9.1k msgs/s
Upstream batch fan-out enqueued ~600k notification jobs onto notif-queue in a short burst; enqueue rate jumped from ~4k/s to ~14k/s and stayed elevated
End-to-end notification delivery p99 climbed to 42s — messages sit in notif-queue waiting (queue-wait), per-message processing still ~6ms
Started container redis (routine probe-driven restart ~9m before incident; consumer connections continuous, LLEN history has no gap — adversarial, not a broker outage)

prometheus_metrics.json.note: app_consumer_lag_k ramps 8 -> 141 -> 373 -> 800 over 4m, breaching the 50 SLO ~16x. enqueue (14010/s) far exceeds consume (4865/s), so backlog grows monotonically. CPU ~92% is at the single-threaded ceiling of each replica — adding CPU would not add consumer parallelism; adding replicas would. Redis fast, memory nominal, nodes Ready — not dependency_failure or resource_exhaustion.

annotations.description: app_consumer_lag_k 803 (803k msgs) > 500 (500k) threshold; enqueue ~14k/s vs consume ~4.9k/s, backlog rising ~9.1k/s
annotations.error: ConsumerLag: notifications backlog grew to 800k msgs

ruling_out_keywords: redis uptime continuous LLEN no gap
answer.model_response:
ROOT_CAUSE: The {{SVC}} consumer group is hit by consumer lag from under-provisioned consumers for its inbound load: after an upstream producer flood at 16:01:38 enqueued ~600k jobs and pushed the enqueue rate to ~14k msgs/s, the 3 {{WORKLOAD}} replicas can only drain a combined ~4.9k msgs/s (each pinned at its single-threaded ceiling of ~1.6k/s, CONSUMER_CONCURRENCY=1). Inbound exceeds drain by ~9.1k/s, so the notif-queue backlog grows monotonically — app_consumer_lag_k climbed 8 -> 141 -> 373 -> 800 (800k msgs) in 4 minutes, ~16x over the 50k SLO and past the 500k alert threshold. This is throughput saturation of the consumer group (too few parallel consumers for the offered load), not a crash, OOM, bad deploy, or broker outage.
ROOT_CAUSE_CATEGORY: saturation
VALIDATED_CLAIMS:
- app_consumer_lag_k = 803 (803k msgs) vs the 50 (50k) SLO, ramping 8 -> 141 -> 373 -> 800 over 4m; backlog rising ~9.1k msgs/s [evidence: prometheus_metrics]
- enqueue rate exceeds consume rate: enqueue 14,010/s far exceeds combined consume rate 4,865/s; each of the 3 replicas is at ~1.6k/s, its single-threaded ceiling [evidence: prometheus_metrics]
- A ProducerFlood event shows an upstream batch fan-out enqueued ~600k jobs and raised enqueue from ~4k/s to ~14k/s [evidence: k8s_events]
- Logs from {{POD}} show 'ConsumerLag: notifications backlog grew to 800k msgs' and 'cannot keep up at current replica count (3)', with redis BRPOP 1.4ms and per-message handler 6ms — the bottleneck is parallelism, not Redis or handler speed [evidence: k8s_pod_logs]
- ConsumerLag alert firing since 16:02:30 with value 803 [evidence: prometheus_alerts]
NON_VALIDATED_CLAIMS:
- Not a dependency_failure: app_redis_brpop_latency_p99=1.4ms and app_redis_up=1; the 42s delivery latency is queue-wait time while messages sit in the backlog, not slow processing by Redis [evidence: prometheus_metrics]
- Not a bad deploy: {{WORKLOAD}} revision 7 is unchanged, last rollout 11d4h ago, image v2.7.4 stable, all 3 replicas Available; the lag coincides with the producer flood, not a deploy [evidence: k8s_deployments]
- Not resource_exhaustion: consumer CPU ~92% is at the single-threaded ceiling (adding CPU adds no consumer parallelism), memory is 41%, 0 restarts, and all nodes are Ready with headroom [evidence: prometheus_metrics, k8s_pods, k8s_node_health]
- Not a broker outage: the redis-0 'Started' event ~9m prior was a routine probe restart with continuous consumer connections and no gap in LLEN history [evidence: k8s_events]
CAUSAL_CHAIN:
- An upstream producer flood pushes the notif-queue enqueue rate to ~14k msgs/s
- The 3 consumer replicas drain at only ~4.9k msgs/s combined (each maxed single-threaded at ~1.6k/s)
- Inbound exceeds drain by ~9.1k/s, so the notif-queue backlog (LLEN) grows monotonically
- app_consumer_lag_k climbs past 50k SLO to 800k and end-to-end delivery latency balloons to 42s (queue-wait)
- The ConsumerLag alert fires; the lag will not drain without more consumer parallelism
- Scaling the consumer group from 3 to 6 replicas raises combined drain above the ~14k/s inbound, the backlog falls, and app_consumer_lag_k returns below the 50k SLO
